coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and properties of carbon quantum dots and their research progress in cancer treatment

Lin, Simin
Huang, Xiaozhen
Jin, Yanqiao
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new type of fluorescence carbon nanomaterials with particle size varied from 2 to 10 nm, have attracted much attention due to their strong multi-color luminescence properties, unprecedented biocompatibility, excellent aqueous dispersibility and easy preparation from a wide range of carbon materials. These multiple attributes help CQDs possess a broad range of prospective applications in numerous fields, especially for bio-imaging, drug delivery, chemical probe and photon therapy because of their advantages of low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Therefore, it is necessary to recap the synthesis and application of CQDs-based materials in the field of biomedicine. The methods for the synthesis of CQDs are introduced firstly, and then different performance of CQDs related to their applications are demonstrated and discussed, including the function tailoring of CQDs. Finally, future opportunities and challenges of CQDs-based materials in this burgeoning field are proposed from the perspective of the rational design of systematic CQDs-based materials toward the biomedicine field.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and properties of carbon quantum dots and their research progress in cancer treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new type of fluorescence carbon nanomaterials with particle size varied from 2 to 10 nm, have attracted much attention due to their strong multi-color luminescence properties, unprecedented biocompatibility, excellent aqueous dispersibility and easy preparation from a wide range of carbon materials. These multiple attributes help CQDs possess a broad range of prospective applications in numerous fields, especially for bio-imaging, drug delivery, chemical probe and photon therapy because of their advantages of low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Therefore, it is necessary to recap the synthesis and application of CQDs-based materials in the field of biomedicine. The methods for the synthesis of CQDs are introduced firstly, and then different performance of CQDs related to their applications are demonstrated and discussed, including the function tailoring of CQDs. Finally, future opportunities and challenges of CQDs-based materials in this burgeoning field are proposed from the perspective of the rational design of systematic CQDs-based materials toward the biomedicine field.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer, as one of the leading cause of death in the world has become the greatest threat to human health [1]. Considering the high risk and mortality of cancer, studies have been conducted on the foundational and clinical research to explore effective and feasible diagnostic method, as well as treatment strategies. Currently, various aggressive malignant tumors spread among people of all; these malignant tumors are usually removed by surgery, radiotherapy and chemotherapy. Although these strategies do succeed in some optimistic therapeutic efficacy, they also suffered from the ignored adverse effects, for example, severe toxicity to normal tissues, drug resistance and tumor metastasis [2–14]. These limitations are difficult to ignore and somehow responsible for the failure of these treatment strategies. Nevertheless, they are also driving the development of more precise and effective treatment strategies to fight against cancer.

Over the past few decades, the fast development of nanotechnology has ushered new ground in areas of nano-medicine. A variety of novel remedial forms have appeared due to the efforts to meet the challenges of biomedical faced by traditional medicine. Among them, noninvasive and minimally invasive therapeutic model has become the centre of attraction in cancer therapy owing to their particular spatial resolution, raised therapeutic results, and reduced adverse side effects [15,16]. Recently, CQDs, acknowledged as an indispensable nanomaterial, have received widespread attention thanks to their unique characteristics, including excellent solubility, favourable biocompatibility and surface modification flexibility [17]. Subsequently, a large number of CQDs have been synthesised by designing various precursors and received substantial applications, such as photocatalysis [18], light-emitting diode [19], ion detection [20], especially nanomedicine.

At present, nanotechnology is increasingly gaining a place in clinical medicine, particularly in the field of oncology [21]. Nanoparticles, such as the combination of QDs and magnetic nanoparticles as magnetic CQDs, are emerging as a contrast agent to be used in medical imaging platform, for instance, magnetic resonance imaging [22] and computed tomography [23]. Moreover, despite the limited ability to generate 1O2, it has been proven that some CQDs can be utilized as photosensitizers (PSs) for photodynamic therapy (PDT), where PSs can transfer light energy to adjacent oxygen [24,25], thus, induced the generation of reactive oxygen (1O2), such as singlet oxygen to kill tumors. Additionally, CQDs can play a role in fields of drug delivery as well as a chemical probe, and thus, we speculated that one potential application of CQDs is cancer treatment.

Here we will individually introduce the latest developments in the synthetic routes (Section 2), diverse performances (Section 3, mainly focusing on low cytotoxicity and fluorescence properties), and the fundamental role of CQDs in the cancer treatment system (Section 4). Then, some unresolved problems and potential opportunities are proposed in Section 5.

Recently, laser ablation has been widely used to prepare CQDs because it can synthesise different nanostructures with easily controlled morphology [39,52,53]. Sun et al. synthesised CQDs from a mixture of cement and graphite powder using argon as a carrier gas [54]. Subsequently, CQDs were found to possess the merits of controlling the size by adjusting the laser pulse size [53] (Fig. 2 A–F), which affected the nucleation and growth of CQDs. The laser beam interacted with graphite and generated a high-pressure steam/plasma plume at the interface between graphite and the surrounding liquid medium, accompanied by instantaneous high temperature (Fig. 2G-step 1). Due to the limitation of the liquid, the bubbles were caused by the laser plume in the laser. When the laser pulse width was too large, the foam began to shrink owing to the pressure, and the inner area cooled, resulting in the fabrication of nuclei (Fig. 2 G-step 2). Additionally, bubbles with different cluster densities were formed by changing the laser pulse width, and then CQDs of different sizes were produced. (Fig. 2 G-step 3).

The electrochemical oxidation method usually refers to the preparation of CQDs by oxidising carbon source electrode via an electrochemical method. Current reports mainly focus on synthesizing CQDs with multi-wall carbon nanotube graphite, graphene and carbon black as working electrodes, showing the advantages of high yield, mild reaction conditions and controllable size [45,47,55–57].

Zhou et al. synthesised first fluorescent CQDs from multi-walled carbon nanotubes by electrochemical method [57]. Subsequently, Kang et al. reported an electrochemical method that assisted by alkali to prepare CQDs with excellent optical and electronic properties, providing a new strategy for high-quality preparation. This process occurred by cutting graphite honeycomb into ultra-fine particles via electrochemical oxidation, and CQDs with various sizes were obtained. Herein, the control group showed that the alkaline environment was necessary for the synthesis of CQDs. Besides, the preparation of highly crystalline CQDs produced from graphite electrodes by electrochemical oxidation under the condition of alkaline was also reported [56]. Interestingly, the aqueous solution of CQDs was colorless but gradually turned bright yellow after being stored at room temperature, which might be attributed to the oxygenation of surface species over time.

Chemical oxidation is a method for CQDs preparation by oxidising and cutting macromolecular carbon materials with strong oxidants, owning the advantages of convenience and high efficiency [35,37,58,59]. Recently, considerable efforts have been made to synthesise CQDs from various carbon sources by chemical oxidation. Huo et al. employed activated carbon as the carbon source and developed an effective route to simply fabricate photoluminescent CQDs, which can be mass-produced. In particular, the raw materials were easily etched into individual CQDs by treatment with nitric acid, and then a passivation process was performed by amine-terminated compounds. Amine-terminated compounds can be used as passivation agents for CQDs. The passivation effect can increase the fluorescence intensity and QY of CQDs. The obtained CQDs are amorphous as no identifiable lattice structure was observed. And additionally, the resulting CQDs showed a narrow size distribution ranged from 2 to 6 nm [59].

Ultrasonic waves can generate alternating waves in liquids, resulting in the formation and bursting of vacuum bubbles. These cavitations cause high-velocity impinging fluid jets, depolymerization, and strong hydrodynamic fluid shear force [41,57,60]. Thus, the ultrasonic energy generated in this process can cut macroscopic carbon materials into nanosized CQDs. Then it was reported that CQDs were synthesised by using an H2O2-assisted ultrasonic method. Surprisingly, no precipitation of nanoparticle was found after six months of storage [57]. In addition, Park et al. fabricated green CQDs from waste food by ultrasonic treatment [41], and the TEM results revealed the average size of CQDs was less than 4.6 nm.

Microwave is a green and efficient method to break the bonds with electromagnetic energy. This approach can provide an uniform and simultaneous heating, resulting in uniform size distribution of CQDs [42–44,61]. Surprisingly, CQDs with stable and bright photoluminescence (PL) were first prepared by microwave method and obtained with excellent dispersion in an aqueous solution [61]. To be specific, different amounts of sugar and PEG200 were added into deionized water to form a clear solution, then heated under 500 W microwave for 2–10 min; as the reaction time increased, the aqueous solution changed from colorless to brown, verifying the synthesis of CQDs. Liu et al. used citric acid, l-cysteine and dextrin as raw materials to fabricate highly fluorescent CQDs by simple microwave heating. The average particle size of the obtained CQDs was 2.61 nm, and they were further used to detect Cu2+ [62].

Thermal decomposition has been previously reported for the synthesis of different magnetic nanomaterials and semiconductor. Previous studies have shown that external heat markedly promotes the carbonization and dehydration of organic matter, thereby converting it into CQDs [44,46,63]. Possessing the advantages of mass production and low-cost, and tailored properties of CQDs by controlling the degree of decomposition. The synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphite QDs by using ethylene-diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) as carbon sources at a carbonization temperature of 260–280 °C was reported [46]. In the solid-phase reaction, the specificity produced by decarboxylation of EDTA may fuse-together gradually. The nitrogen present in the system can withstand high temperatures and be further transformed into nitrides. Robert synthesised CQDs by thermal decomposition using citric acid derivatives as carbon sources and measured the average particle size of CQDs by TEM. After 0.5h of the reaction, the particle size of CQDs was 5–15 nm; 2h after the reaction, the particle size of CQDs increased to about 30 nm and showed a wide distribution [64].

Hydrothermal treatment is a general method to fabricate CQDs from sugar or biomass raw materials with the advantages of low cost and simple operation [50,51]. Chen et al. developed a rapid hydrothermal method for synthesizing CQDs from lignin with the assistant of H2O2 [65]. The mixture of lignin and H2O2 was hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for different times to form a yellow solution and then further filtered, dialysed, and freeze-dried to obtain CQDs with particle size varied from 2 to 10 nm. Moreover, Hua et al. used ethylenediamine, dimercaptosuccinic acid and chitosan to prepare fluorescent CQDs, which could be used for the PDT targeted for mitochondria and mitochondrial imaging without modifying other ligands [66]. Sun et al. synthesised CQDs from citric acid and polyethyleneimine by simple one-pot solvothermal method (160 °C, 4h). Then the synthesised CQDs self-assembled with PSs (Ce6) and decorated with Cu2+ to obtain Cu/CC NPs to construct a tumor microenvironment (TME) stimuli-responsive [67]. To be specific, the nanocomposite showed fluorescence quenching under neutral conditions (human body's normal environment). The FL imaging, PDT and chemical dynamic therapy functions of Cu/CC NPs were selectively activated only in tumors due to the lower pH and over-expressed GSH and H2O2 stimulation of TME, and thus induce the enhanced photo-therapy to further damage tumors and reduce the side effects.

Recently, template method has been widely used in the synthesis of CQDs because it can prepare numerous nano-materials with high QY and uniform size distribution. Yang et al. presented an unprecedented soft-hard template method toward photoluminescent CQDs from organic molecules using ordered mesoporous silica (OMS) SBA-15 as the hard template, and the copolymer Pluronic P123 was used as the soft template. In this report, the organic precursors were enwrapped into the micelles of P123 in an embryo mesostructured silica via a modified synthesis of 3D SBA-15, and the target CQDs were obtained by carbonization templating and passivation [68]. Interestingly, this approach allowed the easy-adjusted of the size and graphitisation degrees of CQDs by selecting different precursors.

Cytotoxicity of CQDs has been a hot topic and attracted considerable attention. Studies showed that CQDs have low toxicity in vitro and vivo [69–74]. It was reported that carbon soot was used to fabricate microgram-sized and water-soluble CQDs by nitric acid oxidation. The synthesised CQDs can directly enter the cells without any modification. Moreover, the cell viability was measured on HepG2 cells by 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) and Trypan blue assay. The cell survival rate was 90–100% at a concentration of less than 0.5 mg/mL for 24 h, which suggested a minimum cell death [70].

High-PL N-doped CQDs (1.3–2.1 nm) were prepared and used as the fluorescent probes. It was worth noting that the as-synthesised N-doped CQDs have negligible cytotoxicity to HeLa cells in the concentration range of 50–400 μg/mL (Fig. 3 A), and the high cell viability confirmed the low cytotoxicity and excellent photo-stability [71]. The cytotoxicity of luminescent N-doped CQDs that using floated molecules as the nitrogen and carbon sources to C6 glioma cells was also assayed using the MTT method, and similar results were obtained. Even if the concentration of CQDs was as high as 300 μg/mL for 24 h, the cell survival rate remained above 86% (Fig. 3B) [72]. The intrinsic cytotoxicity of CQDs prepared from orange juice by using hydrothermal treatment was evaluated by MTT test using the L929 cell line. The results showed that despite the high dose of 200 μg/mL after three days of incubation, the CQDs did not produce any toxic cells and could be tolerated, indicating that CQDs were safe to use in vivo and in vitro [73].

Moreover, naked CQDs had no significant toxicity to human colorectal adenocarcinoma (HT-29) cell models and Michigan Cancer Foundation-7 (MCF-7) even at the concentration of 0.4 mg/mL (Fig. 3C) [74]. However, when CQDs were inactivated by poly propionylethylenimine-co-ethylenimine (PPEI-EI), the cytotoxicity is higher than PPEI-EI at the same concentration. In addition, the CQDs made from soot that passivated by polyethylene glycol (PEG1500 N) under the excitation wavelength of 440 nm were also investigated. The cytotoxicity of CQDs passivated by PEG 1500 N to MCF-7 and HT-29 cells were measured by Trypan blue method and MTT assay. Surprisingly, the survival and death of the above cells indicated that the fluorescent CQDs were non-toxic to the cells.

Male mice were employed to evaluate the toxicity of CQDs in vivo. Some functional parameters of mice in CQDs and the nontoxic control group of 0.9% NaCl aqueous solution such as alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and uric acid (UA) were at similar levels (
Fig. 3
D). The results of serum biochemical analysis showed that high-dose of CQDs (20 mg/kg body weight) had no apparent toxic effect on mice. Histopathological analysis showed that the organ structure of the exposed mice was normal, which was almost the same as that of the control group [69].

Since CQDs are a new type of carbon nanomaterials newly developed in recent years, the origin of the fluorescence emission is still controversial and needs to be further clarified. QDs with different physical and chemical properties can be fabricated by choosing different synthesis methods and precursors. Thus it is necessary to further research to demonstrate the luminescence mechanism and improve the optical properties of CQDs.

The so-called stability of CQDs is that the PL emission remains stable under continuous excitation. CQDs can be synthesised in two different ways, "bottom-up" and "top-down", and most of them possess excellent photostability, which played a crucially role in biological imaging. It was reported that CQDs synthesised from glucose, amino acid, and other monomers have good photostability; that is, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was almost unchanged under 1h excitation, retaining 90% of the original intensity. In addition, the PL intensity of CQDs was more stable than dyes, such as CdTe-QDs, fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) dyes, and polymer nanoparticles reported previously [75]. The preparation of trichromatic emitting CQDs was also be investigated [76]. The CQDs exhibited stable green, blue and red emission under single ultraviolet light excitation and owned excellent light stability in ethanol solution. The attenuation rate was less than 5% after continuous ultraviolet light (UV = 365 nm) for 1h. Interestingly the up-conversion fluorescence characteristics were also observed. Ge et al. prepared red-emitting QDs using polythiophene derivatives as precursors and studied their photo-stability [77]. The fixed cell image of CQDs-labeled HeLa cells showed vigorous red fluorescence, which could be distinguished even after laser irradiation for 2h. The commonly used fluorescent dye FITC dropped to an undetectable level within 10 min, indicating that the CQDs have high photostability in vitro.

Due to the existence of defects, the radiative recombination is reduced, and QY of most CQDs is low, which limited their application [78]. Great efforts have been made to overcome this drawback to improve the luminescence of CQDs through chemical and physical methods. Colon et al. purified the CQDs mixture by high-resolution anion-exchange high performance liquid chromatography; the PL QY was improved to about 7% [79].

Studies have shown that passivation and N, S-doping were efficient methods for enhancing the QY. One thing is that the surface of CQDs could be oxidised or reduced by passivation treatment, and then CQDs were coated with organic polymers and diamine compounds. The reduction of CQDs by NaBH4 was also investigated, and the QY was enhanced from 2% to 24%. Meanwhile, the blue shift of the maximum emission wavelength occurred under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm [80]. Under a nitrogen atmosphere, in the presence of a mixture of sulfuric acid and nitric acid, petroleum coke was oxidised to synthesise CQDs and then functionalized by hydrothermal ammonia treatment. The fluorescence quantum efficiency and fluorescence lifetime were almost doubled, resulting in improved in fluorescence performance [81]. The average particle size of CQDs prepared by Tan et al. through nitric acid oxidation of renewable plant dust was 3.1 nm, and it was evenly dispersed in the solution [82]. The anti-photobleaching experiments revealed that the CQDs showed favourable photostability compared with Rhodamine B and conventional organic dyes. Additionally, the CQDs were modified with passivation ligands, such as poly-l-lysine, 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine, cysteine and poly-l-lysine, and thereby increasing the QY of CQDs by 5.94, 1.53, 3.68 and 2.00 fold, respectively.

To maximize and stabilize the fluorescence of CQDs, an effective method was to dope CQDs with ZnO or ZnS [83]. In the suspension of zinc acetate and CQDs, the zinc acetate was hydrolysed into zinc hydroxide by NaOH, and zinc hydroxide was converted into ZnO by thermal annealing. ZnO doped CQDs with a QY of 45% was obtained. Similarly, ZnS-doped CQDs were obtained by precipitation of the suspension of the zinc acetate and CQDs with Na2S without any thermal annealing treatment. The authors speculated that doping played an meaningful role in surface passivation or improved of surface passivation when combined with organic passivator. Interestingly, under two-photon excitation at 800 nm, the doped CQDs emit bright fluorescence in the visible region, suggesting promising potential in imaging applications. Zhang et al. increased the QY from 9% of N-free CQDs to 31% of N-doped CQDs by heteroatoms doping [73]. It was speculated that the modulation of the electronic and chemical properties of CQDs caused by N-doping might lead to efficient fluorescence emission.

CQDs can be regarded as nano-sized graphene or GO with particle size less than 10 nm, similar to graphene in optical properties, especially PL [84]. Besides, CQDs are considered as emerging fluorescent labels and resistive to photo-bleaching, as revealed by CQDs labeled cell fluorescence imaging under longed illumination [85]. Moreover, CQDs have no toxicity to the dyeing and imaging of living organisms [69]. And most importantly, CQDs are ultra-compact fluorescent probes which had extremely small footprints [86]. All this merits facilitate the application of CQDs in the field of biological imaging.

CQDs have also been widely used in vitro cell imaging [87,88]. The fluorescence CQDs collected by ultraviolet and blue light were used to label Ehrlich ascites carcinoma (EAC) cells, and microscopic imaging was performed (Fig. 4 A), which provided a new basis for the application of CQDs in vitro imaging [70]. The fluorescence characteristics of CQDs provide convenience for cell imaging. Furthermore, the CQDs can enter cells without any modification, which is convenient for tracking their position in cells.

In addition, CQDs could be functionalized and thus endowed it with more excellent fluorescence in response to specific ions, especially metal ions. Full-color CQDs were prepared, which exhibited surprising parallel emission intensity in almost the whole visible spectrum (Fig. 4B) [89] and can act as multi-color biomarker in cell imaging. The CQDs nano-conjugated with amino TPEA was also be reported and this hybrid system was further used for intracellular imaging and sensing of Cu2+ [90] (Fig. 4C). When the CQDs were hybridized with fluorescent CdSe/ZnS, which was inert to Cu2+, the nano-hybrid could form a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor and the emission wavelength ranged from 485 nm to 644 nm. That is to say, the color of the nano-hybrid changed from blue (dominated by CQDs) to red (dominated by CdSe CQDs) when Cu2+ ions were added (Fig. 4D) [7]. Similar ratiometric intracellular fluorescent were also developed to probe Fe3+ [91] and O2− [92].

CQDs were also applied to live animal imaging in addition to cell imaging. Sun et al. reported for the first time the application of CQDs in optical imaging in live mice [93]. CQDs were injected subcutaneously around the back skin, the fluorescence imaging results at 545 nm (green) and 620 nm (red) under excitation at 470 and 545 nm were observed, respectively. The fluorescence tracing results of subcutaneous injection of CQDs into axillary lymph nodes showed that CQDs remained strong fluorescence in vivo, which provides a observably potential prospect for imaging and related biomedicine research.

The existing research on the excitation wavelength of CQDs probes mainly focuses on the blue region, which hinders the application in live-cell imaging. Li et al. reported a fluorescence-on probe based on CQDs for the highly selective determination of selenium in living cells [94]. High PL CQDs (Y-G-CDs) were prepared using m-aminophenol as the carbon precursor. Next, YG-CDs and 2,4-dinitrobenzenesulfonyl chloride were covalently functionalized and used as nanoprobe (CD-DNS) for selenol (Fig. 5 A). Then the DNS moiety of CD-DNS was cleaved by selenolate through a nucleophilic substitution reaction upon treating with selenocysteine, resulting in highly fluorescent Y-G-CDs. This is the first report of a CQDs-based selenol fluorescent probe. The probe can work under physiological conditions, which indicates that CD-DNS can be used as an effective tool for fluorescence imaging of endogenous, exogenous and endogenous alcoholic selenium in living cells.

The direct biological imaging of endogenous ascorbic acid in living cells using near-infrared graphene QDs nanoprobes was reported for the first time. The near-infrared GQDs possessed two-photon PL characteristics. Under 810 nm fs pulse excitation, the near-infrared emission was at 660 nm (Fig. 5B) [87]. In such a nano-system, near-infrared GQDs (NGs) exhibit minimal noise in the living system, thereby improving the resolution of fluorescence imaging. In this work, the synthesised NGs have been successfully used for endogenous live-cell imaging and deep tissue imaging, and the proposed nanoprobe can provide a new platform for in vitro and in vivo analysis of biological targets (Fig. 5C).

Later, Wang et al. used manganese phthalocyanine as the carbon precursor to synthesise magnetofluorescent Mn-CDs, and then self-assembled with DSPE-PEG to improve the aqueous solubility. The as-fabricated Mn-CDs can be used as an intelligent contrast agent for near-infrared and T1-weighted magnetic resonance fluorescence imaging (Fig. 5D) [95], which showed that the maximum emission peak appeared at 745 nm under the excitation of 690 nm, which endowed Mn-CDs the capability of NIR imaging.

Studies have shown that the organic dye conjugated CQDs was an effective H2S fluorescent probe. In the presence of trace amounts of H2S, the blue luminescence of dye-conjugated CQDs changed to green in the process of Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Fig. 5E) [96]. It has been proved that H2S possessed the ability to penetrate cell membrane by simple diffusion [97]. In addition, the ability of organic dye-conjugated CQDs to evaluate the physiologically related changes in H2S levels was investigated using fluorescence microscopy. Particularly, the sensor was used to track H2S level on HeLa and L929 living cells by exposing the sensor-stained cells to H2S (30 or 100 mM) at 37 °C for half an hour. The intracellular fluorescence turned green, indicating that CQDs-based sensor were prominent probes for H2S tracing in living cells. Based on these results, we can conclude that CQDs are of great significance for imaging.

Sustained-release preparations can control the release of drugs and have attracted widespread attention. CQDs possess good biocompatibility and safety and can be used for the monitoring of drug release. Jing et al. synthesised multi-functional core-shell capsules from different materials by coaxial electrospray. Olive oil was used as a reservoir for oil-soluble drugs, magnetite (Fe3O4), carbon (graphene) quantum dots, and dual-layer porous titania (TiO2) shell was used as a sensitive vehicle. CQDs encapsulated in the multifunctional carriers to provide optical information for in-situ monitoring of drug release (Fig. 6 A) [98]. The capsule system played a crucial role in drug release, especially for controlled release and water-insoluble drug release. Considering that CQDs are fully absorbed, combining CQDs with various compounds can achieve drug delivery to targeted tumor sites.

Tang et al. constructed a drug delivery system based on FRET and CQDs (FRET-CQDs-DDS) through a direct surface coupling strategy, where CQDs act as the drug carrier as well as the donor of FRET pairs. Besides, the obtained FRET-CQDs-DDS system possessed excellently targeted drug delivery capabilities in addition to high drug release efficiency for cancer cells in an acidic environment. Under the excitation conditions at 810 nm, FRET-CQDs-DDS was used for two-photon imaging and drug release monitoring of tumor tissues of 65–300 mmol/L, indicating CQDs could be used to monitor drug release in deep tumor tissues (Fig. 6B) [99]. Similarly, the synthesis of therapeutic nano-drug by coupling oxaliplatin on the surface of CQDs was also investigated. The whole particle (CQDs-Oxa) entered the cancer cell through endocytosis, effectively controlling the release of the drug, and the tumor size decreased with the extension of the treatment time. On the way, the conjugate possessed bioimaging capabilities both in vivo and in vitro (Fig. 6C), facilitating the tracking of drug molecules by monitoring fluorescent signals [100]. Karthik et al. constructed a light-responsive nano-drug delivery system by covalently anchoring light-triggered anticancer drugs on the surface of CQDs [102]. The drug-CQDs conjugate was facilely integrated into HeLa cells and accumulated in the nucleus. The conjugate could accurately control the release of drugs to kill cancer cells after irradiation.

CQDs have been developed as nano-carriers for gene delivery. Liu et al. reported a simple one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis of the mixture of glycerol and branched PEI25k. The remaining PEI not only enhanced the fluorescence intensity but also endowed positive charges on the surface of CQDs. The mechanism was rough as follows: By the electrostatic interaction between DNA and PEI [103], DNA condensed on the surface of CQDs to form a complex, which was internalized by HepG2 cells and showed higher gene expression than that of control PEI. Meanwhile, Hu et al. employed branched polyethyleneimine (bPEI) as the carbon source to synthesise bPEI-based CQDs with outstanding biocompatibility through oxidation and hydrothermal reactions. Experiments illustrated that the transfection of the gene in 293T cells could be mediated and visualised by bPEI-based CQDs [104]. However, the toxicity and transfection efficiency of PEI were closely related to the molecular weight [105]. To address this problem, Cheng et al. synthesised polycation-b-polysulfobetaine block copolymer-grafted CQDs, which were used as the vector of gene delivery and exhibited superior hemocompatibility and significant inhibition of protein absorption compared with PEI25k. The nanomaterials could condense plasmid DNA into nanospheres with a size of about 50 nm, which were almost non-cytotoxic in a high proportion [105]. The CQDs in the transfected cells displayed excitation-dependent fluorescence emission, demonstrating that the block copolymer modified CQDs was a promising therapeutic material with good stealth.

Liang et al. constructed a mitochondrial oxidative stress amplification for cancer therapy. They prepared CQDs from citric acid and polyene polymine by a facile hydrothermal treatment and modified them with Au, cinnamaldehyde (CA) and triphenylphosphine (TPP). While CA produced reactive oxygen species and TPP targeted mitochondria, Au and S reacted to generate Au–S bond that consumed GSH, and simultaneously generate reactive oxygen species, which triggered the death of mitochondria and ultimately led to tumor cell apoptosis (Fig. 6D) [101].

Using CQDs as a fluorescent probe to detect small biological analytes is attractive. The fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) prepared by one-step pyrolysis of glutamic acid using for detecting amoxicillin were also evaluated because amoxicillin could effectively separate N-CQDs and thereby reduce the non-radiative, which in turn increased the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 7 A) [106]. CQDs coated molecularly imprinted sol-gel shell can also be used to detect imprinting [107]. The content of nicotinic acid in human urine was successfully determined when using such composite material as a fluorescent probe, and CQDs can also be used to detect dopamine [49], glucose [108] et al.

There are some reports in this field. For example, Liu et al. used candle smoke as a precursor to synthesise CQDs through a hydrothermal reaction. The surface of CQDs was rich in hydroxyl groups, which were beneficial to combine with metal ions, leading to the aggregation of CQDs and quenching of fluorescence [110]. Under optimal conditions, the detection limit of the probe for Cr3+ detection was 60 nM. Moreover, CQDs can also be functionalized by PEG200 and N-acetyl-l-cysteine (NAC) [111]. The prepared CQDs were naturally negatively charged, and the layer-by-layer assembly method was used to promote the deposition of alternate films with cationic polyelectrolytes. Then the film was fixed on the tip of the glass fiber to form an optical fluorescent probe. Interestingly, the terminal residues of NAC could specifically bind to Hg2+ to form a non-fluorescent complex, which resulted in the fluorescence quenching of CQDs. And thus, the probe could selectively detect Hg2+ at sub-micromolar levels, while the interference of other ions was negligible. Yan et al. prepared CQDs with high QY using citric acid as the precursor. Surprisingly, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs could be effectively quenched by Hg2+. Therefore, the CQDs could be used as a selective and sensitive detection in both aqueous solutions and living cells [96]. Moreover the quenched fluorescence was reversible and could be restored by adding ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) to make these probes reversible (Fig. 7B) [109]. Yazid et al. reported the synthesis of fluorescent CQDs from preformed sago starch nanoparticles by carbonization and surface oxidation. The CQDs obtained could be used as optical probes to quantify Sn(II) ions in aqueous solutions. The optical probe showed high selectivity to Sn(II) ions and was hardly interfered by other ions. Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity showed a good linear relationship with Sn(II) concentration [112].

CQDs as chemical probes are usually used for the detection of metal ions and play an irreplaceable role in most case of biochemical process. Therefore, a sensitive and simple method for the detection of metal ions is of great significance. And the continuous development of CQDs technology will inevitably bring new development opportunities to the field of analytical chemistry. In addition, further research and new methods will open up a broad application prospect for CQDs in the field of analysis.

CQDs have good dispersibility and excellent optical properties, such as high fluorescence intensity and stability, easy bio-coupling and strong resistance to photobleaching, making CQDs potentially useful for PDT. This therapy utilises O2 to generate cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS), and is becoming a promising cancer treatment method owning to its non-invasiveness, high efficiency and considerable accuracy [113]. Among the emerging cancer treatment methods, PDT surpasses traditional methods (radiotherapy, surgery and chemotherapy) because it is non-invasive essentially and has few side effect, which leads to negligible drug resistance and low systemic toxicity [67]. In PDT, PSS (Ce6, ICG and so on) produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as 1O2 under illumination to kill the cancer cells. CQDs can be used as a carrier for loading Ce6, which leads to prolonged blood circulation in the nanosystem and also improves water solubility [110]. Moreover, the singlet oxygen generated by CQDs can also be transformed to Ce6 by FRET. Especially, a polymer composed of mPEG, protoporphyrin and tumor target was combined with the as-produced CQDs-doped C3N4 nanoparticles to improve the deficiency of tumor hypoxia and the effect of PDT [113]. As shown in Fig. 8 , the laser of 630 nm can stimulate the decomposition of water into oxygen, thereby enhancing the generation of ROS.

In addition to PDT, photothermal therapy (PTT) has been proven to be controllable, non-invasive and has the function of eliminating cancer cells. In addition to being bioactive to a certain extent, functional biomedical materials can also improve photothermal performance. Additionally, the desired functions can be integrated by bearing in mind the risk assessments.

To further confirm the photothermal conversation performance of CQDs, chitosan has also been reported as the precursor [114]. The covalent bonding system of chitosan and CQDs exhibited spherical morphology in shape with an average diameter of approximately 65 nm. In contrast, in the absence of NIR irradiation, DOX release was lower, while the release rate under NIR irradiation increased by nearly 20% [114] (Fig. 9 ).

Many researchers have devoted to themselves to developing magnetic materials with photo-thermal functions, which are composed of CQDs and magnetic nanoparticles. The core-shell nanoparticles (FeN@CQDs) with iron nitride as the core and CQDs as the shell were also prepared by the hydrothermal method [113]. The nanoparticles showed good dispersibility in aqueous solutions with the spherical morphology, and the average particle size was 6.2 nm. Additionally, the temperature elevation of the nanoparticles irradiated under NIR in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) rose quickly and finally with an increase of 27.6 °C. In contrast, the control group (PBS) increased only 5.8 °C, varying the excellent photothermal property of the nanoparticles, which showed great potential in PTT.

Moreover, the synergistic therapy of PDT and PTT has turned into another hot spot following chemo-photothermal therapy study in cancer treatment, and it has attracted particular attention. Lin et al. prepared a synergistic anti-cancer nano-system with intelligent response through the functionalized PS (Ce6) and Cu2+ and investigated its practical application as a photo-thermal agent. With the change of concentration (50–200 mg/mL), the temperature of the nanosystems rose from 7.7 to 15.98 °C, while the control group only 0.38 °C increase, and the photothermal conversation efficiency was 45.7%. In PDT, singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG) was used as probe to measure the performance of Cu/CC nanoparticles, and the results showed the excellent effect of PDT [67].

Numerous synthesis approaches of CQDs have been discussed, so more and more CQDs are manufactured in a low-cost and easy-to-synthesise way. CQDs have the advantages of environmentally friendly, favourable biocompatibility and excellent fluorescence characteristics, etc., which broaden the application field. It is exciting to witness that the research interest in CQDs has shifted from traditional fields of energy storage to emerging fields, such as biosensing, devices and cancer treatment. Accordingly, we have attempted to make a general review of the latest research on CQDs, focusing on the synthesis, properties and multiple roles in cancer treatment. To date, various portable, size-controlled, cost-effective and mass-produced methods have been developed to prepare CQDs with different structures and compositions, endowing them with different properties. In particular, the low cytotoxicity together with excellent fluorescence characteristic of CQDs endowed it an obvious merit in vivo biomedical applications. Owing to these fascinating features, CQDs have now become a potential competitor of the conventional semiconductor QDs as bio-imaging agents. Even CQDs have better performance because of the favourable biocompatibility and the ability to be decorated with various parts. After the modification of therapeutic compounds and the conjugation of targeting, CQDs can be released precisely, which holds great potential to address the challenges of side effects and adaptability. Moreover, different functional groups on CQDs can be combined with different metal ions, making them suitable candidates for chemical probes. Additionally, the potential phototherapy can destroy tumor cells to a maximum extent without damaging normal tissues. Thus, CQDs possess the technical potential to develop new precise diagnostics and effective therapeutics, leading to a shift in the treatment patterns towards cancer. These biomedical applications, including bio-imaging, chemical probes and photo-therapy, can not only provide new ideas and basis for CQDs to be used in tumor therapy, but also improve the therapeutic efficiency, which is of great significance to social development and human health.

Previous studies have shown that CQDs can be used to treat cancer in many different forms, and phototherapy is one of them. It can selectively destroy tumors while minimizing damage to normal tissues by precise irradiation with an adjustable laser dosage. Phototherapy includes PDT and PTT. Among them, PDT, which has been clinically approved by the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA), is considered as one of the most efficient therapeutic modalities owing to its non-invasive and precise therapy towards diverse cancer. PTT has fascinated scientists for decades because it avoids molecular oxygen dependency and has exceptional advantages, including minimal side effects, convenience and ultra-high efficiency. Therefore, the CQDs are expected to achieve clinical applications in cancer therapy. Although significant progress has been made in this field in the past decade, there are still many issues worthy of further study, as follows:
(1)
The fluorescence intensity of CQDs plays a vital role in cell imaging. However, the QY of CQDs currently synthesised is still very low. Therefore, more efforts should be devoted to improving the carbon QY.
(2)
There has been numerous research on the functionalization of CQDs so far. However, simple and controllable modification of QDs is still a lead issue, which will open the door to better fluorescence quantum efficiency in numerous field.
(3)
Theoretical basis and experimental research further explain the mechanism of CQDs related performances. Thus, more struggles conducted on the mechanism are desired to make full use of this feature.
(4)
Most of the CQDs used in cancer treatment (such as imaging) display a strong absorption in the infrared absorption region, indicating the need for longer emission wavelengths. Specific CQDs can be prepared through N, S-doping or surface modification to obtain emission wavelength in the NIR region, facilitating cell imaging during cancer treatment.
(5)
The biological effects of CQDs in cells and animals and their mechanisms are still unclear, which need to be analyzed using metabolomics.

Although some issues have been solved, we will still face many exciting challenges and opportunities in the near future to explore the enormous potential of CQDs in various technical applications. It is hoped that through the review of this article, we will provide rich information about the multiple roles of CQDs in the rational design of cancer treatments to promote rapid development and ultimately achieve efficient, long-term and stable treatments.
